Signal→Interpretation→Fan action→Recovered demand
Industry brief
A high-level view of how onTour improves demand behavior, fan outcomes, and revenue performance across major tours.
Fans don’t care how the system works.
They care whether they can get a ticket.
onTour gives them visibility, options, and a next move before they enter the on-sale.
Why this matters now
The issue isn’t only market structure. It’s how fans experience the system.

Fans don’t see contracts, integrations, or operating complexity. They experience one outcome: whether they had a fair, understandable path to a ticket.

When they don’t, the system feels opaque, unfair, and impossible to navigate — regardless of how it is actually built.

That perception fuels frustration, media cycles, political pressure, and regulatory scrutiny.

1
Visibility
Fans see real pressure and access paths before the on-sale instead of guessing blind.
2
Options
Markets, dates, travel paths, added shows, returned tickets, and adjacent live opportunities become actionable.
3
Better experience
The fan journey shifts from blind competition to informed decision-making — without changing ticketing, pricing, or control.
The issue
The system sees demand. It doesn’t shape how fans act on it.

Industry platforms already understand their own transactions, inventory, pricing, and sell-through better than anyone. onTour is not trying to replace that view.

It operates outside the transaction layer — where fans form expectations, compare markets, decide whether to travel, and choose what to do after they miss.

Super Bowl
Controlled access, staged releases, premium scarcity, clear expectations.
Formula 1
Travel demand, destination behavior, hospitality, multi-day monetization.
Major tours
Fans often hit the peak on-sale with long odds and no clear second move.
Fan rage is often an expectation-management problem. The fix is not pretending every fan can win — it is showing them the pressure, the options, and the next move early enough to act.
What onTour does
Same demand. Different behavior.

onTour changes what fans do before and during on-sale: where they focus, when they act, what they avoid, and where they go next if the first attempt fails.

1
Shows pressure before the queue
Fans see when their home market is brutally oversubscribed instead of learning only after they lose.
2
Surfaces actionable options
Better dates, backup markets, added-show watches, travel paths, and re-entry windows become visible early.
3
Keeps intent alive
A failed fan is still high-intent. onTour gives that fan somewhere useful to go next.
What this is not
onTour is not a discovery platform, ticketing app, or resale marketplace.
It operates before the on-sale and throughout the tour — helping fans decide where to compete, when to act, and what to do next so demand distributes more intelligently across markets, dates, and access paths.

The money
You monetize the spike.
onTour monetizes what doesn’t convert.
Every sold-out show leaves behind unmet demand. onTour converts that frustration into incremental revenue opportunities across the tour and the broader live ecosystem.
1
Markets
Travel-ready fans see where tickets are still accessible, creating music-tourism value beyond the hometown market.
2
Dates
Added shows, returned-ticket windows, and late inventory become alertable demand events.
3
Ecosystem
Support acts, collaborators, related artists, smaller shows, club nights, and theme events reach fans while intent is still hot.
Key takeaway
Demand doesn’t disappear.
It gets redirected — and captured.
onTour does not pull fans away from primary events. It captures demand that would otherwise go unconverted.
How it works
A simple guidance layer above the live-event system.
onTour reads observable demand signals — official listings, on-sale timing, inventory movement, access paths, and tour changes — with API partnerships as the long-term integration layer.
1
Tracks observable demand signals
Official listings, on-sale windows, access paths, event status, inventory signals, market patterns, and tour changes.
2
Detects what changed
Added dates, timing shifts, status changes, inventory movement, cross-market divergence, and related-event activity.
3
Guides fan decisions
Best-shot markets, avoid zones, backup dates, alerts, travel options, re-entry windows, and ecosystem recommendations.
Close
The next growth layer is not more demand.
It is better demand behavior.

Superstar demand already exists. The opportunity is the missed fan, the travel-ready fan, the fan waiting for another date, and the fan still looking for a live night out.

onTour does not compete with ticketing. It converts frustrated demand into incremental revenue opportunities.
onTour publishes before the queue, stays active during on-sale, and keeps working throughout the life of the tour.

Fan protection
Protect yourself before the next move.
If you don’t get tickets, the next move matters even more. Track rule changes, resale risks, and scam red flags before you spend real money.
Six reasons fans are furious — and what the data actually shows
Buying risk
Low odds can push fans into bad decisions.
Don’t rush into risky resale listings. Screenshots, PDFs, DMs and “transfer later” offers can leave you with a useless ticket.

🛡️
Protect Yourself
Buying tickets shouldn’t mean risking your money.
Know the rules, the risks, and the red flags before you buy.
What’s changing
Ontario caps resale at face value
Ontario’s new fan-first ticket law bans resale above the original face value for events in the province. FIFA temporarily pulled Toronto World Cup resale listings while reworking its marketplace to comply.
Reuters CityNews
Scam alerts
Red flags before you pay
  • Screenshots are not tickets. Barcodes can be copied, reused, or cancelled.
  • PDFs can be duplicated. The first scan may win; everyone else loses.
  • “Transfer later” is a major risk. Don’t pay before you understand when and how transfer opens.
  • DM sales and e-transfers offer little protection. Once money moves, recovery can be difficult.
  • Too-good-to-be-true pricing usually is. Extreme discounts are often bait.
Buy safer
What to check before buying resale
Guarantee
Use platforms that guarantee entry or buyer protection.
Transfer method
Verify mobile transfer rules before sending payment.
Jurisdiction
Rules can change by province, state, country, venue, or event.
Source
Avoid off-platform deals when you cannot verify the seller.
onTour position
Intelligence before risk
onTour doesn’t sell tickets. It helps you understand your options — before you enter the queue, and before you make a risky move after missing out.
Protect yourself after a miss.
High-demand tours can push fans toward risky resale. Know the red flags before you pay.
onTour
Tour Ticket Intelligence™
A fan intelligence layer for high-demand live events
onTour helps fans understand demand, availability, and how tours actually behave — before they ever enter the queue.
Most ticketing platforms show what is available. onTour shows what is likely to happen, where fans may have a better shot, and what to track as the tour evolves.
Your Best Shot
A pre-on-sale read that compares markets and gives fans a practical next move: try local, consider another market, or track added dates.
Tour Pattern Intelligence
onTour looks at previous sellouts, added dates, market demand patterns, multi-night cities, and international availability to understand what may happen next.
Track This Tour
Tracking a tour includes demand signals, added shows, new markets, inventory shifts, and artist-connected opportunities — without asking fans to manage separate watchlists.
Related Artists
Artists shown here are connected to the tour — collaborators, support acts, or direct creative orbit — not random recommendations.
Better Market Paths
For some tours, another city or country may offer a better access path. onTour helps fans compare options before the on-sale, not after it is too late.
Strictly opt-in alerts
Text alerts are strictly opt-in. Fans choose whether onTour watches near them, travel-worthy dates, or all major tour updates.
How the experience works
1
A major tour is announced
onTour publishes a featured tour page before the on-sale, when fans are deciding where and how to try.
2
Fans get their best-shot read
The tour page compares markets, expected pressure points, and realistic access paths using available tour intelligence and previous tour behavior.
3
The read is grounded in patterns and data
Previous sellouts, added-show behavior, market concentration, multi-night runs, international availability, and available tour supply data help inform what is likely to happen next.
4
Fans track what matters
Track This Tour includes added shows, new markets, inventory shifts, and connected artist activity — all anchored to the artist the fan already cares about.
5
Alerts stay in the fan’s control
Fans can keep tracking inside the app or explicitly opt into text alerts with their chosen scope: near me, travel-worthy dates, or all tour updates.
Why it matters
Superstar tours can behave more like global destination events than normal retail transactions. Demand can exceed supply by multiples, local dates may be impossible, and another market may offer a better path.
The core promise:
Know your best shot before you enter the queue.
onTour does not change ticketing. It changes how fans experience it — by making demand understandable, surfacing real opportunities, and helping fans make better decisions before it is too late.
Prototype note: Best Shot market reads currently combine available tour supply data, modeled patterns, and illustrative demand logic. Live demand, inventory, predictive scoring, and SMS delivery are coming with data integrations and messaging partners.
